sentences.text: Well, the name is Arie Gedis.
The old name was Gekdishman.
G-E-D-I-S.
And the old name was G-E-K-D-I-S-H-M-A-N.
Well, I was born in Rovno, which is Volhynia, in Poland.
And it was the 12/12/1918.
My father's name was Samuel.
Mother's name was Rachel.
Buchvah.
Yes.
I had three sisters.
There's Esther and Sara and Miriam.
They were younger.
He was a tailor, a high class tailor.
Yeah.
Middle class family, yes.
No.
We were Jewish.
We observed the holidays, observed everything that is Jewish.
But I wouldn't say we were very religious.
It was a holiday, every holiday-- Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, you know, you felt-- Purim, Hanukkah, you always felt the atmosphere of the holiday.
And we celebrated it, yes.
And we went to shul.
Passover was kosher.

Used to clean the dishes according to the old laws, you know, outside in a hot little-- digging a hole, and hot water, and washing and cleaning them, you know, and taking out the special-- the, the, the dishes, you know, for Passover.
Yeah, we did that.
Yeah.
Yes.
It was fine.
It was fine.
We didn't understand much about religion.
But we had some friends over there coming with their parents too.
So we went, too, you know?
And later, we start understanding what religion is really, you know?
Well, it was-- in this neighborhood, in this part of Poland-- it was a, a bigger town compared to where we lived, a big town, you know?
Had some Jewish people sport.
There was a, a gymnasium.
There were schools, a lot of schools, and everything.
Sport was very much developed int he Jewish community, you know?
And it was a bigger town.
Yes.
We had everything.
Yeah.
It was pretty large.
Yeah.
Well, the earliest memories, I used to go-- in the beginning-- I used to go to a rabbi, you know, when I started growing up, when we were a kid, little kid, you know, to a rabbi.
And used to teach us, you know, how to read, about the [INAUDIBLE], and all those little things, you know?
I remember that.
He used to, used to watch us and say repeat again and repeat again, you know?
So that was the beginning.
And now, I start growing up, you know?
I-- my parents brought me into a school, you know.
They enlisted me in a school, Tarbut, which the main language was Hebrew.
That's why I started to learn Hebrew in those days, yeah, as a kid.
I went there for quite some years.
Yeah.
And this was still in Rovno?
No.
No.
That was-- we left Rovno when I was a little kid, you know?
My parents left Rovno.
And they moved. 12 kilometers from Rovno was a little town named [?
Zdolbonov. ?] My father thought that in this town, he might do some business, you know, and establish himself.
And in such a big town like Rovno, you know, a smaller town will be more interesting, you know?
And that's where we lived, about 12 kilometer from Rovno.
Yes.
Zdobonov-- ?] Z-D-O-L-B-O-N-O-V.
Well, we had a good life.
And the life in school was wonderful, you know?
We had a Jewish education.
And Hebrew was important, you know?
We also had little sport games we used to play with the non-Jewish people.
And of course, we had sometimes fights, you know, because they didn't like when they lose, you know?
We had some fights.
It was fun, you know?
Yeah.
Especially basketball, you know?
We liked basketball.
That's what we played.
Later years, when I grew up a little, we start playing soccer.
That was the game, you know?
What other activities were you interested in as a young boy?
Depends what age you mean, a young boy.
I was-- I remember once in school, you know, I was a young kid.
And we had some art.
And I draw a fork, just a fork that you eat with.
And the teacher looks at it, looks at it, looks at it, and says, you know something?
This is very nice.
He say, you've got talent.
I say, a fork?
What does a fork say about talent?
He says, well, it's, it shows that you are very good, you know, in it.
How old were you then?
I would say about 10, 11 years old.
And then later years, of course, I start thinking, you know?
I start doing things, you know, blah, blah, here and there, you know, start drawing.
And things used to come out, you know?
Whatever I thought, you know, I used to put it on paper.
And that was very interesting.
Later-- the other thing that interested me was music, you know?
We had a little radio.
You can't-- you couldn't hear far away, you know, just close by, one station, you know?
And that's the time when I learned about Wagner, Brahms, you know, Beethoven, and all this.
And I knew-- I learned all those things.
And I liked it, you know?
I liked it.
And later on, said to my father-- he says, what do you want to be when you grow up?
Say, you know, I would like to be the guy with that, with that thing, you know, that shows the orchestra how to play.
He says, what?
Are you crazy?
He said, why don't you start with something learning, you know, how to repair shoes or being a good tailor, or something that you make some money and keep up your family?
I say, I like that.
He says, well, now, this is not for you, and-- in those days, people thought a little different about the culture, you know?
And what about school?
What kind of a student were you?
I was average student, average student.
And then I did what I have to do, you know, and try the best.
But because I had a little interest in other things, you know, it took away my attention to other things, too, you know?
I would say no.
Because we went to Jewish, Jewish Hebrew school, you know?
And most of the friends were Jewish, you know?
For the evening, the holidays, and the, in the, in the temple and all that stuff, you know, you met mostly Jewish people.
We were apart.
And this little town, you know, we had very religious Christians and Protestants, you know?
And they stayed apart from us.
And we stayed apart from them.
We lived our own culture.
It was nearest close to Rovno, 12 kilometer.
Well, Rovno was considered the largest city in this reg-- region, you know?
Yeah.
There was Rovno, Kovno, and other cities.
But this was considered as a big cultural city, you know?
Yes.
For instance, my sister wanted to go to a school, a non-Jewish school to learn all kind of subjects that we don't have in the Jewish school.
And they came out with numerus [INAUDIBLE] which is in Latin that nobody is allowed on numerus clausus which is a limited-- kind of people can go to school, you know, not everybody if you're Jewish.
You know, out of 100, maybe 10 could go to school.
And numerus [INAUDIBLE] means that nobody, no Jews, are accepted.
And that was the first thing.
You know, we start learning about anti-Semitism.
In those days, no.
I had a radio, very cheap radio.
And that's the only thing that kept me very interesting, you know, about music, especially music, you know?
Well, it depends what kind of problems.
You are asking me about the family problems or personal problems?
Yes.
We talked about it.
Nobody imagined that the situation is so tense, you know?
And we never knew what's in his mind.
Because it was a start of his party.
We knew that he is a Jew hater, you know?
Was an anti-Semite.
We knew all these things.
But then we had in Poland many of them, too.
So we didn't know what really-- how far they'll go with it, you know?
And only later, we start finding out.
What was your home like?
Describe it to us.
It was not a big home.
And we have a little corner in the backyard where I used to play, you know?
And my sisters used to fight around a little bit, like any family, you know?
But we liked each other.
And mother and father were good to us, real good.
They did what they can.
In those days, we didn't need so much luxury as we need today.
And we loved them.
My mother was a good cook.
And every Friday night, we had to be together sitting down at the table and have a good time.
My mother was a little strict.
But my father was good.
And she screamed at me.
She give me once a little bonk.
He says, here is 25 [INAUDIBLE].
Go buy yourself a orange.
A orange was a big deal in those days, you know, buy yourself a orange.
He liked to keep the kids, you know, in this way.
She was a little straight.
But we loved her.
It wasn't because she was a bad mother.
And she taught [? about ?] us and do the best she can.
I remember it was a big chaos in those days.
I didn't know the truth of the, of the terrible situation, what it is.
Because young people, you know, they don't think too much.
Well, so what?
They came in.
So what?
Who cares?
So what?
And it start getting very, very serious.
And then when they stepped into Poland-- the Russians stepped in, in our part of Poland.
[INAUDIBLE] How did your life change at that time?
Well, it changed a lot, you know?
They start looking right away, you know?
They send their people to the army against.
And they brought in their laws and all that stuff, you know?
But they were looking for young people, which they wanted to send away to Russia to take place of the people that they took to the army.
So they needed young people, you know?
And that's when the tragedy started, really, you know?
Well, one day they showed up in our house.
Is that your son outside there playing?
My mother says, yes.
OK.
We want to take him with us.
She says, where?
When?
Where?
What for?
He says, well, we're taking him with us.
He's going to work for us.
They didn't tell her where they're going to take me, what they're going to do with me, nothing.
We're going to take him with us.
He's going to work for us.
She ran first to my father, you know?
She said, they want to take away me, you know, and all that stuff.
And my father says-- he runs out outside near the door.
They say, well, we need him.
And this is a law.
This is the law, Stalin's law, and that we have to do.
We have to.
I say, I want to take some things with me.
Nothing-- don't take nothing with you.
Just the way you are, we'll take you.
I was surprised, you know?
I was scared.
I didn't know the, the reality of the situation.
And I say to my mother, mother, don't worry about it.
I'm going to work over there for them.
They're going to do, going to do some things.
Everything will be fine.
And my mother was standing outside of the door, you know?
And I remember she was-- she had tears in her eyes.
Said, why are you crying, mother?
She say, I'm afraid.
I'm afraid you're not going to see me anymore.
And I'm not going to see, not going to see you anymore.
My father kept her hand and says, don't worry about it.
It's a war.
We have to fight the wars.
He will be all right.
He's young.
And that's when they took me away.
That time, I was about 18 years old-- 17, 18 years old, yeah.
And that's when they took me away.
And that's when I saw the last time my parents.
Well, after we left, you know, we didn't know.
We thought, really, they want us in this place somewhere around there in the neighborhood or a little further.
But then they send us away to, to the station, you know, to the trains.
Yeah.
I knew some people, too, yes.
And then they took us away.
They took us [? weeks and weeks. ?]
We were traveling about over a week.
It took weeks to go further down.
But the first time we stopped, we stopped in the Ukraine.
And they give us places to work, you know, to dig in anti-tank, you know, the dividers that the tanks couldn't go through, you know?
And We worked on it.
And everybody had to make so much, you know?
This is what you got to do today.
And this is what you got to do tomorrow.
And if you don't do that, you won't get food today.
So we did.
I was young.
I was strong, you know?
And I did.
What was it--
That's where they kept us for some weeks.
We slept outside.
Yeah.
They gave us something to cover up, you know?
We slept outside.
It was mostly in, in a place where they had trees, you know, like a-- you know, trees were growing out, you know?
In the places like that, we used to sleep.
Then after a short while, they took us all.
We have to go buy food.
So it took us about over two weeks to walk, you know, with them.
I had with me a friend.
He wasn't a friend.
I didn't know-- I never knew him.
But he was from a little town somewhere in the Ukraine.
And he got sick and couldn't walk.
And the order of Stalin was in those days, they can't walk, just leave them there.
Shoot them.
They needed people here to work.
If they are sick, we don't need them.
That was the law.
I felt bad about this kid, you know?
I was strong, thanks to God.
I-- and I was able to give him a hand, put my hand under his waist, you know?
And I carried him.
I went with him.
I wanted to save him.
Maybe he'll feel better tomorrow.
Then winter came.
Things were cold, didn't have enough to wear things, you know?
It was very cold.
We made miles and miles.
I don't know how many.
But we walked there days and weeks, for a few weeks.
Finally, they took us to a train.
And then were all strangers.
I never saw all there one of the kids that I left my home town.
They took us to that train.
That was the last of the traveling, you know, that we traveled, you know?
That's [? the whole ?] [? voyage. ?] Did they feed you?
Did the Russians feed you along the way?
Did they--
Very, very badly, very badly, just to stay alive, just to stay alive.
The reason is because, first of all, they don't know how to handle it.
Secondly, they were poor, you know?
I mean, they don't know how to deal with this kind of situation.
And we suffered.
The dirt in the train, and all that, you know, for, for weeks-- you know, it's enclosed, no air, nothing, until we arrived to a Siberian town named Omsk.
Well, Omsk is located in Northern Siberia, you know, Siberia.
Cold, you know, Siberia-- the name Siberia gives you, gives you the, the, the, the, give you a cold feeling, you know?
It's cold, very cold.
It's wintertime over there.
It comes to 30 below zero.
And they took us out to a place where they had the wooden barracks, you know, and say, you're going to stay here.
They we had wooden benches, kind of.
You know, that's where you're going to sleep.
And that's where you're going to be.
Now, that wasn't a camp.
Of course, they had some people that watched this place over there.
And that's it.
So we didn't know what we're going to do.
But next day or so, they came and told us, we got a big factory here that is working for the army.
They are producing tires, you know, for the airplanes, for the cars, chains for the tanks, and all that stuff.
You're going to work here.
So we started to work there.
Well, I was with a lot of Russians.
And I was surprised.
I've seen very little Jewish people over there, because they divided all these people in different places.
I met a lot of Russians, you know, very nice people.
But they suffered from the same thing, you know?
Because of the war and all that stuff, they suffered.
Why they suffered, they found out later, you know?
They were people, you know, creators a lot-- artists, musicians, writers, intelligent people.
They-- but you couldn't-- some days you couldn't talk to them.
They were drunk.
They start drinking alcohol.
They were drunk.

And the reason is because they didn't have their freedom to write a book about the life in Russia, to paint a painting without a Kreml-- Kremlin, or to write music without, without Lenin and Stalin, you know, in the-- put it in the libretto, you know?
And that's why, you know, they, they were not good for the Russians.
That's why they were there.
Where did--
And I start writing to put together and all, writing a-- I say, if I, if I be alive and I come home, it's interesting to show my mom and dad, you know, and other people the life what was here.
I still writing about these people.
They were nice people, you know?
They knew about music, about literature, about everything.
And I learned from them.
I looked at them like gods, you know?
Not talking ?] the way they look.
And finally, they start writing a little thing.
And this [? song ?] catches of them, of all these people-- names and all that stuff, you know?
And I liked it, you know?
That was a part of my life over there.
I walked out.
I came home.
And I had to do it, you know very discreetly.
Because Russians, you know, the guards, could see what I do, you know, and this and that.
So I did it, you know?
Before we go too far, let me just ask you.
What were the living conditions like there every day that you were there?
Very bad.
What did you do?
We didn't have enough, enough-- I mean, clothes to wear.
Of course, it's not comparing to a-- later when I heard about the, about the concentration camps.
It's far away from that.
But we didn't have nothing to wear.
The food was bad, very low, bad.
I mean, you couldn't see food with calories or something good.
It was real bad, you know-- a little soup, you know, with a piece of bread.
And, and it was limited.
I remember, I put a piece of bread for tomorrow morning.
You know, when I get up, I'll have few bites.
It disappeared overnight.
You know, somebody stole it below my pillow, you know?
So everybody was hungry.
And what kind of work did you do while you were there?

Just simple work-- we, we didn't know nothing about the tires, how to do tires and make just simple work, the worst work, the heaviest-- the hardest thing they had to do, you know, clean up things, you know, carrying things, pushing things, and stuff like that.
Well, we didn't know what's going on in the world to much, you know?
The Russian papers wrote only what's-- what they wanted to write, you know?
We didn't know about United States, the situation, what the United States thinks about the war, about England, about France.
We didn't know these things.
They didn't write in the papers about those things.
But we knew that Hitler exists.
And he's-- we didn't know about the camps.
No.
No.
No.
You couldn't.
Want to be a traitor?
One, two, three, you know-- why are you writing over there?
You don't have to write.
The KGB was in your [INAUDIBLE], you know?
The KGB used to come in at night and take away people.
And a woman was screaming.
I met a woman.
And she said, they took away my husband in the middle of the night.
I said, why?
They didn't tell me why.
I says, he'll be back.
They took him away.
And they sent him away to a camp.
They had camps over there, you know, in Siberia.
They send him away.
Because he told some friend something that's against the Russian, you know, politics.
So they found that he is anti-Russian, anti-communist, you know?
And they had a lot of people like that.
So you couldn't get in touch with nobody.
You couldn't talk to nobody.
The only thing, the good thing that I found out, you know, is that I learned the language.
And the language is a beautiful language, no question about it, you know?
That's all.
The rest, we got to go from day, day to day, you know?
Is there anyone that you met while you were in this work camp that particularly stands out?
No.
No.
But I heard about somebody that was sent to Siberia in a camp, you know, and was sent away and did something wrong.
Or he said something wrong.
No.
They didn't let you.
They changed you every time the places.
But the people I knew that I wrote about them, I knew them very well.
And they liked me, too, you know, because I was interested.
And I respected their, their knowledge about certain things, you know, that they really liked it.
Yeah.
But I couldn't stay with them too much.
Because they did all right.
They didn't want you to get too much involved with other people, you know?
So they change, change around.
How did they change you?
What did they do?
They tell you tomorrow, you work over there.
And you work over there.
And you work over there, you know, different-- it's a big place, you know, big factory, you know?
It's one of the biggest.
That's it.
Well--
A little, a little romance I had in that time, you know?
Other people used to come over and visit, you know, there and bring some food.
There was a girl over there, a Lithuanian girl, you know, used to come over.
She was interested-- used to come over.
It was times when people could come and visit you, you know?
And she was [? with ?] them.
And kind of, we created a, a little, you know, friendship.
And one day in the evening, she thought she would bring me food, you know?
That was a time when nobody was allowed to come in, you know?
So she thought she would bring me food.
I left with enough and maybe split with somebody else, you know?
So they caught her.
They grabbed her, the, the, you know, soldiers.
And they beat her up so, so strongly, you know?
She was bleeding.
I heard about it later.
They took away the food.
And they, they used it, you know?
We never got this food, you know?
I never seen this girl again, you know?
She was a Lithuanian girl.
And she lived over there in Omsk with her father, a Jewish girl.
I never seen her again.
Oh, what-- I would say, close to 4 years.
No.
No.
They control it.
They check.
Where you going?
What you're going?
Unless you had to go because they told you to go, that's a different thing.
But no.
There wasn't really a camp close, you know?
You could, you could run away, you know, and come back later.
But when they catch you, you know, it's, it's not like a concentration camp, you know?
But it's far away.
Where are you going to run, you know?
The town is in the middle of, of, of, of, you know, winter storms and all that.
You can't go far.
They knew you can't run away nowhere over there.
I'm going to stop you now.
We're going to change the tape and start again.
OK?
Por favor.
